channel.title: todo on mahmood
channel.description: Recent content in todo on mahmood

item.title: mechanics
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{\i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{\j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) <&kleppner1973> 1 true physics vector a vector in physics may differ a bit from that of a math vector a “true” physics vector is independant of the coordinate system we pick while a non-true physics vector may be dependant and its values may change when we change the coordinate system 2 position vector the position of an arbitrary point P at \((x,y,z)\) is written as \[ r = (x,y,z) = x\ihat + y\jhat + z\khat \] the position vector isnt a true physics vector, because it doesnt really have a direction since it just represents a position in space and so the position vector of a specific point in space may differ from one coordinate system to another, as we can pick any coordinate system we want and it wouldnt affect the position of a point 3 displacement vector if we move from the point \(x_1,y_1,z_1\) to some new position \(x_2,y_2,z_2\) then the displacement defines a “true” vector \(S\) with coordinates \[ S = (x_2-x_1,\ y_2-y_1,\ z_2-z_1) \] a displacement vector is a true physics vector 4 instantaneous velocity instantaneous velocity, usually called just velocity is a quantity that tells us how fast an object is moving at a single point in time and space the SI unit for acceleration is metre per second (\( rac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{s}}\)) velocity can also be defined as the derivative of position with respect to time, as it is the limit of the average velocity as the time interval approaches zero: \[ v = \lim_{\Delta t 	o 0}  rac{x(t+\Delta t)-x(t)}{\Delta t} =  rac{dx}{dt} \] using notation due to Gottfried Leibniz, who independently invented calculus.

item.title: symacs
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) symacs, which stands for symbolic emacs is a latex-based interface to sympy built with emacs integration in mind symacs looks for common math writing patterns and parses them into sympy objects to then evaluate them symbolically 1 motivation for a latex-based computer algebra system interface 2 sage vs sympy sagemath is far sure much more powerful than sympy, but sympy is just a python library (about 10mb) and sagemath packs lots of software (including sympy and maxima) that amount to gigabytes of space, so i decided to just go with sympy, which hopefully will be much more powerful in the future as it has a huge community 3 parsing latex math expressions texsoup does all of the work from TexSoup import TexSoup mytex = r'$E_1=\pmqty{1 & 0\\ 0 & 1},E_2=\pmqty{0 & 1\\ 1 & 0},E_3=\pmqty{1 & 0\\ 0 & -1},E_4=\pmqty{0 & 1\\ -1 & 0}$' soup = TexSoup(mytex) print(soup.
